verses.text: And David made him houses in the city of David, and prepared a place for the ark of God, and pitched for it a tent. Then David said, None ought to carry the ark of God but the Levites: for them hath the LORD chosen to carry the ark of God, and to minister unto him for ever. And David gathered all Israel together to Jerusalem, to bring up the ark of the LORD unto his place, which he had prepared for it. Guidance: David sets out a second time to bring up the ark, but now everything is different. Having learned the bitter lesson of Uzza's death, he prepares a place for the ark and declares the principle he had overlooked before: 'None ought to carry the ark of God but the Levites: for them hath the LORD chosen.' This time he searches the word of God to find how the ark was to be moved, and orders all things accordingly. The chapter is showing us a man who has been humbled and corrected, and who responds not by abandoning the good work but by doing it rightly. The first attempt failed because they 'sought him not after the due order'; now David is careful to follow God's appointed way. The chapter is teaching us the right response to a chastening from God: not to give up the work, nor to blame God, but to search His word, find where we went wrong, and obey. The chapter is teaching us that the LORD has appointed means as well as ends; it is not enough to want the ark home — it must be brought home as God commanded, by those whom God chose. The chapter is teaching us to value the word of God as our guide in His service, and to learn humbly from our mistakes. David's renewed zeal, now joined to obedience, is a beautiful picture of a believer who has stumbled, been corrected, and risen to serve God again — this time His way. The presence of God is worth seeking with all our hearts, but it must be sought 'after the due order,' according to the word.

And David assembled the children of Aaron, and the Levites: Of the sons of Kohath; Uriel the chief, and his brethren an hundred and twenty: Of the sons of Merari; Asaiah the chief, and his brethren two hundred and twenty: Of the sons of Gershom; Joel the chief, and his brethren an hundred and thirty: Of the sons of Elizaphan; Shemaiah the chief, and his brethren two hundred: Of the sons of Hebron; Eliel the chief, and his brethren fourscore: Of the sons of Uzziel; Amminadab the chief, and his brethren an hundred and twelve. Guidance: David assembles the priests and the Levites family by family — the sons of Kohath, Merari, Gershom, Elizaphan, Hebron, and Uzziel, each with their chief and their numbered brethren — to take their appointed part in bringing up the ark. The careful ordering by families shows how seriously David now took the doing of God's work in God's way. The chapter is showing us the importance of order and of everyone serving in his appointed place. Where the first attempt was marked by careless enthusiasm, this time each Levitical house is summoned to its proper duty. The chapter is teaching us that the LORD's service is not a disorderly free-for-all but a calling in which each has his assigned part and station. The chapter is teaching us that there is a place for every family and every servant in the work of God — Kohathites, Merarites, Gershomites, and the rest, all gathered, all numbered, all needed. The chapter is teaching us to honour order and appointment in the worship of God, and to take up gladly the particular service to which He calls us. When God's people are rightly arranged, each doing his own part, the work goes forward to His glory; when all is left to impulse and the crowd, even good zeal can go astray. David's careful muster of the Levites teaches us reverence, order, and the dignity of every appointed role in the house of God.

And David called for Zadok and Abiathar the priests, and for the Levites, for Uriel, Asaiah, and Joel, Shemaiah, and Eliel, and Amminadab, And said unto them, Ye are the chief of the fathers of the Levites: sanctify yourselves, both ye and your brethren, that ye may bring up the ark of the LORD God of Israel unto the place that I have prepared for it. For because ye did it not at the first, the LORD our God made a breach upon us, for that we sought him not after the due order. So the priests and the Levites sanctified themselves to bring up the ark of the LORD God of Israel. And the children of the Levites bare the ark of God upon their shoulders with the staves thereon, as Moses commanded according to the word of the LORD. Guidance: Here is the heart of the chapter, and David's own confession of his earlier error: 'because ye did it not at the first, the LORD our God made a breach upon us, for that we sought him not after the due order.' David names the cause of Uzza's death plainly — they had not sought God 'after the due order' — and he calls the priests and Levites to 'sanctify yourselves' before they touch the holy things. And then it is done rightly at last: 'the children of the Levites bare the ark of God upon their shoulders with the staves thereon, as Moses commanded according to the word of the LORD.' The chapter is showing us the difference between the first attempt and the second: the new cart is gone; now the ark rests on consecrated shoulders, carried as God had commanded. The chapter is teaching us the necessity of holiness in those who handle holy things: 'sanctify yourselves.' Those who would bear the ark, who would draw near to God and serve in His worship, must be cleansed and set apart, for God is holy. The chapter is teaching us the supreme principle of acceptable worship: it must be done 'as Moses commanded according to the word of the LORD' — not according to our own ideas, however reverent, but according to God's revealed will. The chapter is teaching us to learn from our breaches and chastenings; David did not excuse the past but confessed it and corrected it. And the phrase 'as Moses commanded according to the word of the LORD' is the golden rule of all true service: God is to be worshiped in the way He Himself has appointed. The shoulders of sanctified Levites, not the wheels of a borrowed cart — that is the difference between presumption and obedience, between a breach and a blessing.

And David spake to the chief of the Levites to appoint their brethren to be the singers with instruments of musick, psalteries and harps and cymbals, sounding, by lifting up the voice with joy. So the Levites appointed Heman the son of Joel; and of his brethren, Asaph the son of Berechiah; and of the sons of Merari their brethren, Ethan the son of Kushaiah; And with them their brethren of the second degree, Zechariah, Ben, and Jaaziel, and Shemiramoth, and Jehiel, and Unni, Eliab, and Benaiah, and Maaseiah, and Mattithiah, and Elipheleh, and Mikneiah, and Obededom, and Jeiel, the porters. So the singers, Heman, Asaph, and Ethan, were appointed to sound with cymbals of brass; And Zechariah, and Aziel, and Shemiramoth, and Jehiel, and Unni, and Eliab, and Maaseiah, and Benaiah, with psalteries on Alamoth; And Mattithiah, and Elipheleh, and Mikneiah, and Obededom, and Jeiel, and Azaziah, with harps on the Sheminith to excel. And Chenaniah, chief of the Levites, was for song: he instructed about the song, because he was skilful. And the sons of Levi the porters were Berechiah and Elkanah, doorkeepers for the ark. And Shebaniah, and Jehoshaphat, and Nethaneel, and Amasai, and Zechariah, and Benaiah, and Eliezer, the priests, did blow with the trumpets before the ark of God: and Obededom and Jehiah were doorkeepers for the ark. Guidance: As the ark comes up, David organizes a great ministry of music and praise: singers with psalteries, harps, and cymbals, 'lifting up the voice with joy'; the chief singers Heman, Asaph, and Ethan; Chenaniah set over the song 'because he was skilful'; priests with trumpets and Levites as doorkeepers. The worship of God is to be joyful, abundant, and skillful. The chapter is showing us that the bringing of God's presence among His people is an occasion for the fullest, gladdest praise, and that David spared no care to make the worship worthy. The chapter is teaching us that the LORD is to be praised 'with joy' — worship is not a grim duty but the glad lifting up of the voice and the offering of music to God. The chapter is teaching us, too, the value of skill in the service of worship: Chenaniah was put in charge of the song 'because he was skilful.' God deserves our best, our trained gifts, our diligent preparation; excellence in worship is a way of honouring Him. The chapter is teaching us that there is a place for many gifts in the praise of God — singers and players, those with cymbals and those with harps, trumpeters and doorkeepers — each contributing to the whole. The chapter is teaching us that joyful, ordered, skillful worship pleases the LORD, and that the music of His people is itself a holy ministry, appointed and organized for His glory. When God comes to dwell among His people, let there be joy, let there be skill, let there be the full-voiced praise of grateful hearts.

And David spake to the chief of the Levites to appoint their brethren to be the singers with instruments of musick, psalteries and harps and cymbals, sounding, by lifting up the voice with joy. So the Levites appointed Heman the son of Joel; and of his brethren, Asaph the son of Berechiah; and of the sons of Merari their brethren, Ethan the son of Kushaiah; And with them their brethren of the second degree, Zechariah, Ben, and Jaaziel, and Shemiramoth, and Jehiel, and Unni, Eliab, and Benaiah, and Maaseiah, and Mattithiah, and Elipheleh, and Mikneiah, and Obededom, and Jeiel, the porters. So the singers, Heman, Asaph, and Ethan, were appointed to sound with cymbals of brass; And Zechariah, and Aziel, and Shemiramoth, and Jehiel, and Unni, and Eliab, and Maaseiah, and Benaiah, with psalteries on Alamoth; And Mattithiah, and Elipheleh, and Mikneiah, and Obededom, and Jeiel, and Azaziah, with harps on the Sheminith to excel. And Chenaniah, chief of the Levites, was for song: he instructed about the song, because he was skilful. And the sons of Levi the porters were Berechiah and Elkanah, doorkeepers for the ark. And Shebaniah, and Jehoshaphat, and Nethaneel, and Amasai, and Zechariah, and Benaiah, and Eliezer, the priests, did blow with the trumpets before the ark of God: and Obededom and Jehiah were doorkeepers for the ark. So David, and the elders of Israel, and the captains over thousands, went to bring up the ark of the covenant of the LORD out of the house of Obededom with joy. And it came to pass, when God helped the Levites that bare the ark of the covenant of the LORD, that they offered seven bullocks and seven rams. And David was clothed with a robe of fine linen, and all the Levites that bare the ark, and the singers, and Chenaniah the master of the song with the singers: David also had upon him an ephod of linen. Thus all Israel brought up the ark of the covenant of the LORD with shouting, and with sound of the cornet, and with trumpets, and with cymbals, making a noise with psalteries and harps. Guidance: Now the ark comes up at last 'with joy,' and the Chronicler notes a precious detail: 'when God helped the Levites that bare the ark.' Even in doing the work rightly, they needed God's help — and He gave it. They paused to offer sacrifices in thanksgiving, and David, the king, laid aside his royal robes and clothed himself in plain linen like the Levites, even wearing 'an ephod of linen,' joining the worshipers as one of them, while all Israel came up with shouting, trumpets, cymbals, and song. The chapter is showing us a king who humbled himself before the LORD, setting aside his dignity to worship with abandon among his people. The chapter is teaching us that 'God helped the Levites' — that even when we serve in God's appointed way, success is His gift; the right method does not earn the blessing, the LORD bestows it. The chapter is teaching us the beauty of humility in worship: David, the greatest man in Israel, dressed not as a king but as a worshiper, putting himself on a level with the Levites in the presence of God. Before the LORD, the highest must become low; true worship strips away our pretensions and lays us bare before God. The chapter is teaching us that the proper response to God's presence is wholehearted, joyful, humble worship — shouting and music and song, the king dancing in linen, all Israel rejoicing. The chapter is teaching us to worship God with our whole selves, laying aside pride and reserve, glad to be counted simply among His worshipers, and to remember that whatever we accomplish in His service, it is because 'God helped' us.

Before the LORD, the highest must become low; true worship strips away our pretensions and lays us bare before God. The chapter is teaching us that the proper response to God's presence is wholehearted, joyful, humble worship — shouting and music and song, the king dancing in linen, all Israel rejoicing. The chapter is teaching us to worship God with our whole selves, laying aside pride and reserve, glad to be counted simply among His worshipers, and to remember that whatever we accomplish in His service, it is because 'God helped' us.

So David, and the elders of Israel, and the captains over thousands, went to bring up the ark of the covenant of the LORD out of the house of Obededom with joy. And it came to pass, when God helped the Levites that bare the ark of the covenant of the LORD, that they offered seven bullocks and seven rams. And David was clothed with a robe of fine linen, and all the Levites that bare the ark, and the singers, and Chenaniah the master of the song with the singers: David also had upon him an ephod of linen. Thus all Israel brought up the ark of the covenant of the LORD with shouting, and with sound of the cornet, and with trumpets, and with cymbals, making a noise with psalteries and harps. Guidance: Now the ark comes up at last 'with joy,' and the Chronicler notes a precious detail: 'when God helped the Levites that bare the ark.' Even in doing the work rightly, they needed God's help — and He gave it. They paused to offer sacrifices in thanksgiving, and David, the king, laid aside his royal robes and clothed himself in plain linen like the Levites, even wearing 'an ephod of linen,' joining the worshipers as one of them, while all Israel came up with shouting, trumpets, cymbals, and song. The chapter is showing us a king who humbled himself before the LORD, setting aside his dignity to worship with abandon among his people. The chapter is teaching us that 'God helped the Levites' — that even when we serve in God's appointed way, success is His gift; the right method does not earn the blessing, the LORD bestows it. The chapter is teaching us the beauty of humility in worship: David, the greatest man in Israel, dressed not as a king but as a worshiper, putting himself on a level with the Levites in the presence of God. Before the LORD, the highest must become low; true worship strips away our pretensions and lays us bare before God. The chapter is teaching us that the proper response to God's presence is wholehearted, joyful, humble worship — shouting and music and song, the king dancing in linen, all Israel rejoicing. The chapter is teaching us to worship God with our whole selves, laying aside pride and reserve, glad to be counted simply among His worshipers, and to remember that whatever we accomplish in His service, it is because 'God helped' us.

And it came to pass, as the ark of the covenant of the LORD came to the city of David, that Michal the daughter of Saul looking out at a window saw king David dancing and playing: and she despised him in her heart. Guidance: As the joyful procession enters the city, one heart is cold. Michal, Saul's daughter and David's wife, watches from a window, sees the king 'dancing and playing' before the LORD, and 'despised him in her heart.' Where all Israel rejoiced, she sneered; where David humbled himself in worship, she thought him undignified. The chapter is showing us the sad contrast between the worshiper and the scoffer — between David, who gladly abased himself before God, and Michal, who valued royal dignity above the praise of the LORD. The chapter is teaching us that there are always those who despise wholehearted devotion, who look down on the joy and abandon of true worship as beneath them. Michal, raised in the house of Saul (the king who 'enquired not' of God), had inherited a heart that prized appearances over godliness. The chapter is teaching us to beware the spirit that scorns fervent worship; it is the mark not of refinement but of a heart far from God. The chapter is teaching us that what looks like foolishness to the proud — a king dancing before his God — is precious in the LORD's sight, and that we should rather be despised by the Michals of this world than ashamed to humble ourselves before God. The contempt 'in her heart' reveals where the real poverty lay: not in David's joyful self-forgetfulness, but in Michal's cold, proud, joyless soul. The chapter is teaching us to choose David's part — to worship freely and gladly, careless of the world's scorn — and to fear the barrenness of a heart that can watch the praise of God and feel only disdain.
